text: The obstacles that a judge has to encounter in trying to balance the principles of legal certainty, justice and utility can be broken down into two levels: internal and external obstacles. Internal obstacles are those of a psychological nature that affect the judicial figure itself and the immediate professional framework in which it is inserted: the selectivity of the recruitment and selection processes; the quality of judicial training; the extent of specialist and legal knowledge; the judge’s personal morality; the judge’s state of welfare. External obstacles on the other hand are those that affect the judiciary in a more structural or environmental way, and therefore it is possible to include the real independence of the judiciary and the absence of political and hierarchical interference; the quality and clarity of the wording of the laws and regulatory provisions; the organisation of the judiciary and efficiency of the administration of justice; the degree of participation and trust of civil society; the efficiency of mechanisms for monitoring the activity of the judiciary. According to this research, obstacles to a fair and coordinated justice system are of great importance.
headline: Fully Human Written
